proposal: Joining from an invite requires an explicit confirm action (POST); loading the invite URL never consumes or grants membership. Password-fallback accounts reject top-common passwords at set time and enforce a sane minimum length; login and recovery endpoints are per-account and per-IP rate limited with backoff, and authentication errors are uniform (no member-enumeration signal).
rationale: The two-minute frictionless join plus optional email maximizes the forwarded-invite risk, so join must be a deliberate act and the public login endpoint must not leak or fold to stuffing.

proposal: The token default is no-expiry (months of use with no re-auth is the requirement; record the default in ADR-003). The token grants read, one-tap react, and email-mediated comment only: no profile edit, no directory contact fields, no member-list browsing, no export, no invite. First open exchanges the URL for an httpOnly session cookie and redirects to a clean URL; Referrer-Policy is no-referrer on every token surface. Tokens are >=128-bit CSPRNG, stored only as a hash, and appear in logs only as a short hash prefix. Token, digest-link, and signed-media routes carry X-Robots-Tag noindex and a robots.txt disallow. Token links cannot be issued to supervised members.
rationale: The token is the credential and the URL is the session, so the ceiling, the URL-to-cookie exchange, and hash-at-rest are the load-bearing pre-code decisions for the elder path.

proposal: The provisioning UI shows the exact pod and yard the token will grant before generation. Regenerating invalidates the prior token and ends with the re-hand-over artifacts in hand: a fresh link ready to send and a fresh printable QR in the same flow. The UI names the surface as the elder path and cannot issue a token to a supervised member.
rationale: Regeneration is the remedy six token threats fall back on, so it must be one visit, not a project, or the residuals silently inflate; mis-scoping and kid-tokens are closed at the mint point.

proposal: Household invites expire by default (days, not never) and carry a use cap sized to the household; they are revocable. The admin can list all outstanding invites and see, per invite, exactly who joined from it and when.
rationale: An unlimited-use never-expiring invite is a standing bearer credential that mints full yard members, and a removed ex re-enters through invites others created unless they are revocable and listed.

proposal: Every object read (posts, comments, media and all derivatives, profiles, directory entries, reactions) routes through one central authorization guard evaluating shared pod/yard membership at request time against current membership, deny-by-default to a 404. 'Does not exist' and 'exists but not yours' return byte-identical status, body, and headers. The automated isolation test matrix covers single-object fetches of every type, search and mention-autocomplete, reactor and commenter lists, admin endpoints (member lists, digest delivery status, token provisioning), media derivative types (original, thumbnail, rendition, transcode, poster frame), and digest rendering including the upcoming-dates section. A read handler that does not pass through the guard fails the build.
rationale: The draft's isolation tests covered feed, members, media, and search only; the single-object path, admin endpoints, derivatives, and the digest are exactly where the central guard classically gets skipped.

proposal: The composer defaults to the narrowest scope (the poster's own pod) on every new post; the last choice may be offered but is never the committed default, and media posts always default to pod. Any send broader than the poster's pod, or spanning more than one yard, requires an explicit confirmation naming the audience and its member count. Every rendered post shows its own audience.
rationale: The sticky remembered choice is the single most likely privacy failure in the product, escalating a kid photo from pod to whole-family without any attacker.

proposal: Delete purges the original and all derivatives in the same job and invalidates outstanding signed URLs at the serving layer (deletion beats expiry via a per-request liveness check). Delete-time copy states plainly in one sentence that copies in already-sent email digests cannot be recalled. Media responses on interactive surfaces carry no-store or short cache headers.
rationale: A parent's act of child protection must actually remove the photo everywhere the instance controls, and the UI must never imply erasure it cannot deliver.

proposal: Metadata stripping happens server-side at ingest for every path media can enter, regardless of any client-side processing, and covers the whole location and identity class (GPS, embedded EXIF thumbnails, XMP/IPTC owner and serial fields), keeping only orientation and capture timestamp. The strip runs before any derived asset is generated. Uploads are re-encoded at ingest and served with an exact Content-Type, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and Content-Disposition.
rationale: Client-side strip is a bandwidth nicety, never the control, and re-encoding at ingest both strips metadata and stops an uploaded SVG or polyglot from executing as active content.

proposal: Video metadata stripping at ingest covers container metadata (QuickTime and MP4 location atoms such as com.apple.quicktime.location.ISO6709), mirroring S-401's photo rule, and runs before any transcode or poster frame is generated. An ingest test corpus of real iPhone and Android video with known location is asserted clean post-ingest.
rationale: Photo-oriented EXIF stripping does not touch video container atoms, which carry precise home, school, and park coordinates that an estranged co-parent can read with exiftool.

proposal: Every media fetch, original or derived, passes a server-side pod/yard membership check against the requesting session or token; unguessable ids are defense-in-depth, never the access control. No static-served derivative directory exists; derivative ids are independently unguessable, not derivable from the source id. Signed media URL TTLs are tiered by surface: interactive feed and token surfaces short (hours), digest-embedded URLs matched to the digest-token lifetime; all carry the member's token-generation id so revocation kills them instantly regardless of TTL. Direct fetch without a valid session or token returns 404.
rationale: Derivatives are the side door that voids both the isolation and deletion promises while the main tests stay green, and untiered TTLs either break late-opened digests or leave media live after revocation.

proposal: The digest is built per recipient through the same audience-resolution code path as the web feed, evaluated against live state at send time, so deleted posts, narrowed audiences, and removed members never ship. Multi-yard members receive separate per-yard emails; no single email fuses two yards. Digest deep links carry per-member, per-digest, read-only, expiring tokens, never the master token. A content-free confirmation must be acknowledged before any digest with family content is sent to a new address. Removal, leave, and death immediately drop the member from recipient lists and cancel queued sends. Isolation tests (S-202) cover digest rendering including the upcoming-dates section and every signed media URL.
rationale: The digest is the highest-volume egress channel with no recall and no 404, so it must never be a second audience engine and must never embed the master token or mail children's photos to an unconfirmed address.

proposal: Reply addresses are >=128-bit-entropy, per-member, per-post bearer capabilities, revoked on any membership change. Attribution comes from the reply address alone; the From: header is only a secondary consistency check that quarantines on mismatch and never attributes. Bounce responses are byte-identical for 'no such thread' and 'not your thread'. Comments created via email carry a visible 'via email' badge. Each per-post reply block in the digest states in plain large text where a reply goes, and a confirmation receipt after posting offers one-tap removal.
rationale: The reply address is a write capability into the family's highest-trust space, and the flagship reciprocity feature otherwise broadcasts what an elder believes is a private one-to-one email.

proposal: The central authorization guard is the mandatory path for every read, write, and grant, including batch and email paths. The permission matrix documents the full credential and channel inventory that removal must clear, names the managing parent as exclusive controller of a supervised account's credentials and conversion (with a notifying instance-admin break-glass), and requires the instance admin to initiate recovery, token minting, or removal for any member whose yard set exceeds the acting admin's scope.
rationale: A yard admin reaching a bridging member's other yard through recovery or token minting is a cross-yard bypass that looks authorized in every log, and the removal channel inventory has to live in the matrix so future features register.

proposal: Removal is one atomic server-side revocation over an explicit channel inventory, each with an automated test: revoke all sessions; disable all credentials including passkeys; revoke elder and display tokens; cancel the digest subscription and any queued sends; deactivate every inbound reply and pod address; void all invites reaching the member's pods and yards; stop issuing signed URLs for them. A revocation-completeness test asserts old token, old session, old digest link, old media URL, and reply address each 404 or bounce on the next request. The same revocation runs on voluntary leave and pod-leaves-yard. Removal enumerates supervised accounts and elder-helper roles the member manages, forces credential rotation, and reassigns custody before it completes.
rationale: Continued access by the exact person the family excluded, especially through a child's account or a surviving digest subscription, is the single worst product failure short of instance takeover.

proposal: Supervised status is visible to everyone in a shared pod roster, not admins only. Supervised surfaces carry no 'private' framing and tell the member plainly that the managing parent can see everything on the account. Supervised accounts cannot attach an email or new auth method and are excluded from self-service credential recovery; recovery flows only through the managing parent. Supervised members cannot be issued token links. Adding a supervised member to a pod, or a supervised member creating one, generates a quiet non-push notice to the managing parent.
rationale: Honesty over pretense keeps teens on the instance rather than driving them to less-safe apps, and the one quiet carve-out ensures Backyard is never the least visible channel for a child.

proposal: The first-run wizard is gated by a one-time setup secret printed only to the server console, and is hard-disabled whenever an instance admin exists, including after restores and upgrades (the enabling condition is 'zero admins exist', never a config flag). There are zero default credentials; all internal secrets are generated randomly at first boot and the app hard-fails on an empty or placeholder secret. The shipped compose publishes only the reverse proxy port, with DB, cache, and queue on an internal network. The bundled auto-TLS proxy makes HTTPS the zero-config default, and the app refuses to mint token links for a non-HTTPS base URL in production. Instance-admin and yard-admin accounts require a passkey or TOTP, enforced in the wizard. The wizard generates a backup passphrase and a printable recovery and succession sheet.
rationale: Security is the default path or it does not exist for this operator class, and admin login is a remote equivalent of server shell over the whole archive, so it is the one place the fifteen-minute bar yields.

proposal: The one backup command encrypts by default with a wizard-established passphrase or keyfile and a printable recovery sheet; plaintext output requires an explicit --no-encrypt flag with a loud warning. The restore drill exercises the encrypted path before each release. Restore ends with a forced security-replay: rotate the token-signing material (with one-click re-issue as recovery) and surface a checklist of removals and revocations that postdate the backup, so restore never resurrects revoked access. The documented flow ends with copying the encrypted file off the server, and backup filenames and manifests carry no secrets.
rationale: An unencrypted backup is the largest single confidentiality loss available, and a naive restore silently re-arms a removed abuser's access to children's photos at the moment attention is lowest.

proposal: As the family admin, I can mark a member as passed away, a humane action distinct from removal, so their credentials stop without erasing them. Acceptance: marking a member deceased runs the full revocation inventory (token, sessions, digest, reply addresses) immediately; the digest stops; the profile is frozen out of birthday and anniversary surfaces; content and attribution are preserved with remembrance framing, and the content-disposition choice (keep attributed or memorialize) is made explicitly; the admin runbook names the action.
rationale: Death is near-certain over the instance's life and the orphaned token is severe, but a private alpha of living members does not require it on day one; it reuses the S-702 handler and should land early.

proposal: As the family tech person, I have documented handover, shutdown, and domain-lapse procedures. Acceptance: a one-command decommission revokes all tokens, sessions, and inbound addresses and delivers a final instance export to at least two named members before destroying volumes and provider snapshots; a handover runbook rotates every secret, regenerates all tokens, and transfers registrar and host accounts; the wizard's recovery sheet is a succession sheet naming a second admin, registrar and VPS accounts, and the backup passphrase location; deploy docs treat the domain as a family asset (auto-renew, multi-year, registrar lock) and the health email reports domain days-remaining.
rationale: Single-maintainer abandonment and domain lapse are the likeliest end-states, but the runbook is documentation-weight lifecycle work that a safe alpha does not need shipped before the first family joins.

proposal: As the family tech person, if I lose my admin second factor I can recover admin access only through server shell. Acceptance: break-glass admin recovery is a one-time console command that prints a short-lived reset URL (the same trust anchor as the first-run setup secret), never a web flow; no email or web 'recover admin' path exists; deploy docs recommend configuring a second instance admin.
rationale: Mandatory admin 2FA (shipped in v1) makes lockout probable for a hobbyist, and whichever recovery path exists, or the vacuum where it should be, ships with the first line of auth code.
